summary: DeVault argues that IRC's lack of modern features like embedded images, rich text, threaded messages, and infinite backlog is actually a strength, not a weakness. He contends that these limitations establish a shared baseline that serves users on old hardware, niche operating systems, and accessibility tools. Adding features non-uniformly degrades the experience for users whose clients don't support them, while the constraints naturally foster healthier communication culture. He notes that Slack users' top complaints—distraction, catching-up pressure, unwieldy threads—are caused by the very features IRC lacks. DeVault concludes that IRC's decentralized, volunteer-run, non-monetized nature and its 30+ year longevity make it superior to newer alternatives.
key_points: IRC's constraints create a shared baseline that accommodates users on old hardware, niche OSes, and accessibility tools like screen readers
Non-uniform feature support degrades the experience for those without it—rich text fallbacks are worse than what users naturally do within limitations
The most common complaints about Slack and Discord (distraction, catch-up pressure, unwieldy threads) are caused by features IRC intentionally lacks
IRC's decentralized, volunteer-run, non-monetized nature and 30+ year track record represent genuine advantages no competitor has matched
The migration of non-hackers away from IRC is framed as a welcome 'reverse Eternal September'
money_quotes: I'm glad that IRC doesn't have the features that are "showstoppers" for people choosing other platforms, and I'm worried that attempts to bring these showstopping "features" to IRC will worsen the platform for the people who use it now.

By instead making everyone work within the limitations of IRC, we establish a shared baseline, and expressing yourself within these limitations is not only possible but makes a better experience for everyone.
I think that people who have and think they need them are in fact unhappier for having them.
It's distracting because your colleagues are posting gifs all day.
Long conversations shouldn't be organized into threads, but moved into email or another medium more suitable for that purpose.
You can connect to IRC manually using telnet and have a pretty good user experience!
No one is trying to monetize you on IRC. It's free, in both meanings, and nothing which has come since has presented a compelling alternative.
The culture that it fosters is good and worth preserving, even at the expense of the features users of other platforms demand - or those users themselves.
key_insight: The features modern chat platforms tout as advantages actually create the very problems their users complain about, while IRC's constraints foster a healthier, more accessible, and more sustainable communication culture.
